news_summary: Innovative technologies and community efforts are driving major progress in environmental protection, from advanced water filtration and climate monitoring to revitalizing forestry and rediscovering lost bird species.
news_summary_fr: Les technologies innovantes et les initiatives communautaires sont à l'origine d'avancées majeures en matière de protection de l'environnement, qu'il s'agisse de systèmes avancés de filtration de l'eau et de surveillance du climat, de la revitalisation des forêts ou de la redécouverte d'espèces d'oiseaux disparues.
news_summary_es: Las tecnologías innovadoras y las iniciativas comunitarias están impulsando avances significativos en la protección del medio ambiente, desde sistemas avanzados de filtración de agua y monitorización climática hasta la revitalización de los bosques y el redescubrimiento de especies de aves desaparecidas.

articles.title: Breakthrough water filter removes 98% of toxic PFAS forever chemicals
articles.summary: Scientists have developed a clever new way to trap “forever chemicals” in water using nano-sized cages that lock onto PFAS molecules. Unlike current methods, this approach can capture short-chain PFAS—the hardest type to remove. Tests show it can eliminate up to 98% of these pollutants and still work after multiple uses. The discovery could lead to more effective water filtration systems worldwide.
articles.content:
Contamination from perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) has spread into groundwater, surface water, and even drinking supplies, affecting millions of people around the world.

Researchers at Flinders University have now developed a promising new approach that could help remove some of the hardest-to-capture forms of these long-lasting pollutants from water.

New Method Targets Hard-to-Remove PFAS

The team, led by Flinders ARC Research Fellow Dr. Witold Bloch, created specialized materials known as adsorbents that can effectively capture PFAS. Their method is particularly successful at trapping short-chain PFAS, which are notoriously difficult to remove with current water treatment technologies.

Their findings, published in the journal Angewandte Chemie International Edition, highlight the use of a nano-sized molecular cage designed to act as a highly selective 'PFAS trap'.

"While some long-chain PFAS can be partially removed using existing water treatment technologies, the capture of short-chain PFAS -- which are more mobile in water -- remains a major unresolved challenge," says project leader Dr. Witold Bloch, from Flinders University's College of Science and Engineering.

"We discovered that a nano-sized cage captures short-chain PFAS by forcing them to aggregate favourably inside its cavity. This unusually strong binding mechanism is different from that of traditional adsorbent materials."

How the Nano Cage Technology Works

To make the system effective, the researchers embedded these molecular cages into mesoporous silica, a material that typically does not bind PFAS on its own.

First author Caroline Andersson, a PhD candidate in chemistry at Flinders University, explains that adding the nanosized cage allows the material to remove a wide range of PFAS compounds from water, including those that are especially difficult to isolate.

"The most exciting aspect of this project was that we first conducted in-depth studies of how PFAS bind within the cage on the molecular level," she says. "That allowed us to understand the precise binding behaviour and then use that knowledge to design an effective adsorbent for PFAS removal."

High Efficiency and Reusability in Water Filtration

Laboratory tests showed that the new material can remove up to 98% of PFAS at environmentally relevant concentrations in model tap water.

"The adsorbent also demonstrated reusability, remaining highly effective after at least five cycles of reuse. These results highlight its potential for integration into water filtration systems for polishing drinking water at the final stage of treatment," adds Dr. Bloch.

"This research represents an important step toward the development of advanced materials capable of tackling one of the world's most persistent environmental contaminants," he concludes.

Growing Concern Over PFAS Pollution

PFAS chemicals are widely used in industrial manufacturing, aviation firefighting foam, and everyday consumer products. Over time, they can enter freshwater and marine environments, raising increasing concerns about potential health risks to humans, livestock, and wildlife.

Acknowledgements: The PFAS study was funded by Australian Research Council grants (FT240100330, DE240100664, DP230100587, CE230100021 and FT220100054), and Playford Trust PhD and ATSE Elevate PhD scholarships. The study used facilities including the MX1 and MX2 beamline at the ANSTO Australian Synchrotron, Australian Cancer Research Foundation detector, Flinders Analytical, Flinders Deepthought and the National Facility of the National Computational Infrastructure, and Microscopy Australia, enabled by NCRIS and the government of South Australia at Flinders Microscopy and Microanalysis.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in water filtration technology that can remove up to 98% of toxic PFAS chemicals, including the hardest-to-remove short-chain variants. This advancement addresses a widespread environmental and public health issue affecting millions worldwide, with potential for broad application in water treatment systems. The article provides detailed context on the technology, its effectiveness, and reusability, highlighting a meaningful positive impact on environmental health.
articles.personality_title: New water filter traps 98% of harmful PFAS chemicals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – PFAS are toxic chemicals found in many products and have polluted water sources worldwide. These “forever chemicals” are hard to remove, especially the short-chain types that move easily through water and can harm health.

**What happened** – Scientists at Flinders University created a tiny molecular cage that traps PFAS molecules. This cage is built into a special material that can catch even the hardest-to-remove short-chain PFAS. Tests showed the filter removes up to 98% of these chemicals and can be reused multiple times without losing effectiveness.

**Impact** – This new filter is different from current methods because it uses a strong binding process inside the nano-sized cage to hold PFAS tightly. Removing short-chain PFAS is a major challenge, so this technology could improve water safety for millions of people by cleaning drinking water more thoroughly.

**What’s next step** – Researchers hope to develop this material for use in real water treatment plants, especially as a final cleaning step to make water safer. Further testing and scaling up will help bring this technology to communities affected by PFAS pollution.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new nano-cage water filter from Flinders University can capture 98% of toxic PFAS chemicals, including the hardest types, offering a promising way to clean polluted water.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouveau filtre à eau élimine 98 % des produits chimiques PFAS nocifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les PFAS sont des produits chimiques toxiques présents dans de nombreux produits et ont pollué les sources d’eau dans le monde entier. Ces « produits chimiques éternels » sont difficiles à éliminer, surtout les types à chaîne courte qui se déplacent facilement dans l’eau et peuvent nuire à la santé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université Flinders ont créé une minuscule cage moléculaire qui piège les molécules de PFAS. Cette cage est intégrée dans un matériau spécial capable de capturer même les PFAS à chaîne courte, les plus difficiles à éliminer. Les tests ont montré que le filtre élimine jusqu’à 98 % de ces produits chimiques et peut être réutilisé plusieurs fois sans perdre son efficacité.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau filtre est différent des méthodes actuelles car il utilise un processus de liaison puissant à l’intérieur de la nano-cage pour retenir fermement les PFAS. Éliminer les PFAS à chaîne courte est un grand défi, donc cette technologie pourrait améliorer la sécurité de l’eau pour des millions de personnes en nettoyant plus efficacement l’eau potable.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent développer ce matériau pour une utilisation dans les stations de traitement de l’eau, notamment comme étape finale de purification pour rendre l’eau plus sûre. Des tests supplémentaires et une montée en échelle permettront d’apporter cette technologie aux communautés touchées par la pollution aux PFAS.

**En une phrase** – Un nouveau filtre à nano-cage de l’Université Flinders peut capturer 98 % des produits chimiques toxiques PFAS, y compris les plus difficiles, offrant une solution prometteuse pour dépolluer l’eau.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo filtro de agua elimina el 98 % de químicos PFAS dañinos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los PFAS son químicos tóxicos que se encuentran en muchos productos y han contaminado fuentes de agua en todo el mundo. Estos “químicos para siempre” son difíciles de eliminar, especialmente los de cadena corta que se mueven fácilmente en el agua y pueden afectar la salud.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad Flinders crearon una pequeña jaula molecular que atrapa las moléculas de PFAS. Esta jaula se incorporó en un material especial que puede capturar incluso los PFAS de cadena corta, los más difíciles de eliminar. Las pruebas mostraron que el filtro elimina hasta el 98 % de estos químicos y puede reutilizarse varias veces sin perder eficacia.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo filtro es diferente a los métodos actuales porque utiliza un proceso fuerte de unión dentro de la nano-jaula para retener firmemente los PFAS. Eliminar los PFAS de cadena corta es un gran desafío, por lo que esta tecnología podría mejorar la seguridad del agua para millones de personas al limpiar el agua potable más a fondo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan desarrollar este material para su uso en plantas de tratamiento de agua, especialmente como un paso final para hacer el agua más segura. Más pruebas y la ampliación de la producción ayudarán a llevar esta tecnología a comunidades afectadas por la contaminación de PFAS.

**Una frase clave** – Un nuevo filtro de agua con nano-jaula de la Universidad Flinders puede capturar el 98 % de los químicos tóxicos PFAS, incluidos los más difíciles, ofreciendo una forma prometedora de limpiar el agua contaminada.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a clear, flowing river gently passing through a glowing, intricately patterned molecular cage made of delicate, translucent nano-structures embedded within porous silica, symbolizing the advanced water filter capturing and holding harmful PFAS particles as tiny, muted-colored spheres, with soft natural tones of blue, green, and earth hues conveying purity and environmental hope.

articles.title: OBSERVER: Five Copernicus apps transforming climate and atmosphere insights
articles.summary:
OBSERVER: Five Copernicus apps transforming climate and atmosphere insights
sonia
Thu, 09/04/2026 - 10:20

From assessing heat and cold stress to exploring historical climate data and future projections or mapping methane plumes and monitoring major aerosol events, a range of user-friendly digital tools from Copernicus is delivering climate and air-quality intelligence to users worldwide. Building on data from the Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) and the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS), these applications translate complex Earth Observation information into interactive maps, time series, and thematic indicators. In this Observer, we present five flagship C3S and CAMS apps which show how open data is being transformed into accessible tools, supporting understanding, risk assessment, and decision-making across sectors and regions.
 
Thermal Trace: monitoring heat and cold stress
Developed by C3S, Thermal Trace is a web application designed to help users assess heat and cold stress conditions in near real time (~5-day latency). It responds to the growing need for health-relevant information as extreme temperature events become more frequent and severe.
The app draws on more than 80 years of climate data from ERA5, the C3S fifth-generation reanalysis of the global climate from 1940 to the present. At its core is the Universal Thermal Climate Index (UTCI), a “feels-like” temperature which integrates air temperature, humidity, wind, and thermal radiation. Understanding how heat stress is measured requires looking into the complex interplay of environmental factors which affect the human body. By reflecting the body’s physiological response to environmental conditions rather than just temperature, UTCI provides a more meaningful indicator of risk than air temperature alone.


C3S Thermal Trace app displaying the number of days with conditions corresponding to at least “strong heat stress” based on the UTCI classification in June-August 2025. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Climate Change Service/ECMWF. 

 
Thermal Trace allows users to explore indicators of cold stress or heat stress. Once they choose an indicator, users can select daily, monthly, seasonal, or annual periods, specify specific locations or custom regions, and switch between maps, time series, and downloadable tables, with a step-by-step video tutorial available for those new to the application. Alongside maxima and minima, it includes practical metrics such as the number of heat-stress days, tropical nights and cold-stress days.
Designed for event reconstruction and trend analysis rather than real-time alerts, Thermal Trace enables journalists, planners, and public-health professionals to benchmark recent extremes against historical patterns and better understand evolving thermal risks. The data used in the application can also be downloaded from the Copernicus Climate Data Store (CDS).
For example, users can revisit the conditions from the 2003 European heatwave, one of the most severe extreme-temperature events recorded in Europe, and explore monthly UTCI maxima over Western Europe, highlighting the widespread and persistent conditions corresponding to heat stress during the event.
ERA Explorer: delving into 85 years of global climate data
While Thermal Trace focuses on human thermal comfort, ERA Explorer provides a broader view of long-term climate conditions worldwide. Built on the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, this application makes decades of global climate data easy to explore to both specialists and non-specialists.
Users can click anywhere on the map or search for a specific location to view climatological averages for key variables such as temperature, precipitation, and wind. The base layer can be switched between 30-year average temperature or precipitation, revealing the warmest, coldest, wettest, and driest regions of the planet, as well as large-scale patterns such as the tropical rain belt.


ERA Explorer app showing monthly precipitation climatology for Dublin, Ireland for 1991-2020. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Climate Change Service/ECMWF. 

 
Monthly breakdowns, animated wind fields, and long-term time series add further depth. All displayed data can be downloaded in CSV format, while advanced users can access Python notebooks to reproduce or adapt the underlying analyses. An in-app guide takes new users through the main features.
Powered by cloud-optimised data storage, ERA Explorer calculates statistics in just a few seconds from decades of hourly data, delivering rapid insights while keeping the full scientific dataset openly accessible through the CDS.
 
C3S Atlas: interactive climate projections and reproducible insights
For users seeking to explore future climate change trends alongside historical data, the Copernicus Interactive Climate Atlas (C3S Atlas) delivers global climate projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP), an international scientific programme which brings together climate models from research centres worldwide to produce standardised projections of past and future climate conditions, as well as ERA5 reanalysis and regional downscaled datasets. Since its launch in 2024, it has become a central tool for examining climate projections, observations, and reanalysis data at global and regional scales.
Key variables include mean temperature, accumulated precipitation, and extreme heat days. Regional time series are among the most popular products, complemented by long-term climatological averages and change maps across different periods and global warming levels.


C3S Atlas visualisation showing the rate of change in summer (JJA) daily mean temperature (°C per decade) in 1991–2020, based on ERA5-Land data. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Climate Change Service/ECMWF.

 
Data can be downloaded directly in different formats, supporting both the communication of results to non-specialist audiences as well as in-depth scientific analysis. A recent addition, the C3S Atlas User Tools, improves transparency and reproducibility through Jupyter notebooks and an open GitHub repository, allowing users to recreate indices and visualisations.
With extensive guidance, a helpful video tutorial, and built-in support, the C3S Atlas serves policymakers, researchers, journalists, and educators seeking authoritative and accessible climate intelligence.
 
Methane Hotspot Explorer: tracking major methane plumes from space
Turning to atmospheric composition, the Methane Hotspot Explorer from CAMS focuses on methane, a short-lived greenhouse gas contributing to near-term warming. The app visualises large methane emission plumes detected using satellite observations from the TROPOMI instrument aboard the Copernicus Sentinel-5P satellite.
The Explorer uses machine-learning techniques to identify these emission plumes. These are displayed on a dynamic global map with estimated upwind source locations and indicative source categories such as coal, oil, gas, or solid waste, based on emission inventories.


Through the Methane Hotspot Explorer app, users can view methane plumes from strongly emitting sources in near real time, explore detailed maps, and access data on the estimated location and magnitude of emission sources. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service/ECMWF.

 
While the kilometre-scale resolution does not allow individual facilities to be pinpointed, the tool shows where major emission events are occurring worldwide. Recent upgrades now provide detailed plume shape visualisations, illustrating how atmospheric conditions influence the spread and transport of methane. By transforming satellite data into an accessible monitoring platform, the Methane Hotspot Explorer supports greater transparency of a crucial driver of climate change.
 
Aerosol Alerts: early warnings for major air pollution events
The CAMS Aerosol Alerts service provides daily, forward-looking insights into major aerosol events affecting air quality worldwide. Launched in 2024, the free tool delivers forecasts up to three days in advance, helping users anticipate aerosol episodes linked to dust storms, wildfire smoke, and severe pollution outbreaks.
Built on ECMWF’s Integrated Forecasting System, the service evaluates aerosol optical depth and surface concentrations of particulate matter with diameters of 10 and 2.5 micrometres against reference levels and predefined thresholds. When anomalies exceed these limits, the system triggers high, very high or extreme alerts.


Visualisation of Aerosol Alerts forecast with the CAMS global system at 12:00 UTC on 26 February, 2026.  Credit: European Union, Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service/ECMWF.

 
The interactive homepage features a global map displaying multiple aerosol species simultaneously, including dust, sea salt, sulphate, organic matter, and black carbon, alongside animated wind patterns showing atmospheric transport. Users can customise views, compare forecasts with satellite observations, explore 2D or 3D perspectives, and review forecast performance by region and season.
A personalised notification system allows users to define areas of interest and receive automated email alerts, helping public authorities, health professionals, and individuals to better respond to air pollution events.
 
Powering the apps: cloud-ready data and a growing ecosystem
Behind these applications lies a data infrastructure built around the Climate Data Store and Atmosphere Data Store. These platforms provide free and open access to large collections of quality-assured climate and atmospheric datasets, spanning reanalysis, observations, projections, and near-real-time forecasts.
The integration of Analysis Ready, Cloud Optimised (ARCO) datasets is enabling interactive analysis and visualisation directly in the cloud, allowing apps to deliver rapid insight directly from the underlying data stores without heavy data transfers.
To showcase this expanding ecosystem, Copernicus has launched a dedicated Data Stores Applications Catalogue with a selection of interactive tools featuring data from C3S, CAMS, and the Copernicus Emergency Management Service (CEMS). The repository brings together a wide selection of Copernicus-enabled tools and provides access to new apps built on Copernicus data. 
Together, these digital tools illustrate how open and FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) environmental data can be transformed into accessible, user-friendly, tools and services, strengthening transparency, supporting decision-making, and bringing climate and air-quality intelligence closer to society.

Thu, 09/04/2026 - 12:00
articles.content:
OBSERVER: Five Copernicus apps transforming climate and atmosphere insights

Thu, 09/04/2026 - 12:00

From assessing heat and cold stress to exploring historical climate data and future projections or mapping methane plumes and monitoring major aerosol events, a range of user-friendly digital tools from Copernicus is delivering climate and air-quality intelligence to users worldwide. Building on data from the Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) and the Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service (CAMS), these applications translate complex Earth Observation information into interactive maps, time series, and thematic indicators. In this Observer, we present five flagship C3S and CAMS apps which show how open data is being transformed into accessible tools, supporting understanding, risk assessment, and decision-making across sectors and regions.

Thermal Trace: monitoring heat and cold stress

Developed by C3S, Thermal Trace is a web application designed to help users assess heat and cold stress conditions in near real time (~5-day latency). It responds to the growing need for health-relevant information as extreme temperature events become more frequent and severe.

The app draws on more than 80 years of climate data from ERA5, the C3S fifth-generation reanalysis of the global climate from 1940 to the present. At its core is the Universal Thermal Climate Index (UTCI), a “feels-like” temperature which integrates air temperature, humidity, wind, and thermal radiation. Understanding how heat stress is measured requires looking into the complex interplay of environmental factors which affect the human body. By reflecting the body’s physiological response to environmental conditions rather than just temperature, UTCI provides a more meaningful indicator of risk than air temperature alone.

C3S Thermal Trace app displaying the number of days with conditions corresponding to at least “strong heat stress” based on the UTCI classification in June-August 2025. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Climate Change Service/ECMWF.

Thermal Trace allows users to explore indicators of cold stress or heat stress. Once they choose an indicator, users can select daily, monthly, seasonal, or annual periods, specify specific locations or custom regions, and switch between maps, time series, and downloadable tables, with a step-by-step video tutorial available for those new to the application. Alongside maxima and minima, it includes practical metrics such as the number of heat-stress days, tropical nights and cold-stress days.

Designed for event reconstruction and trend analysis rather than real-time alerts, Thermal Trace enables journalists, planners, and public-health professionals to benchmark recent extremes against historical patterns and better understand evolving thermal risks. The data used in the application can also be downloaded from the Copernicus Climate Data Store (CDS).

For example, users can revisit the conditions from the 2003 European heatwave, one of the most severe extreme-temperature events recorded in Europe, and explore monthly UTCI maxima over Western Europe, highlighting the widespread and persistent conditions corresponding to heat stress during the event.



ERA Explorer: delving into 85 years of global climate data

While Thermal Trace focuses on human thermal comfort, ERA Explorer provides a broader view of long-term climate conditions worldwide. Built on the ERA5 reanalysis dataset, this application makes decades of global climate data easy to explore to both specialists and non-specialists.

Users can click anywhere on the map or search for a specific location to view climatological averages for key variables such as temperature, precipitation, and wind. The base layer can be switched between 30-year average temperature or precipitation, revealing the warmest, coldest, wettest, and driest regions of the planet, as well as large-scale patterns such as the tropical rain belt.

ERA Explorer app showing monthly precipitation climatology for Dublin, Ireland for 1991-2020. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Climate Change Service/ECMWF.

Monthly breakdowns, animated wind fields, and long-term time series add further depth. All displayed data can be downloaded in CSV format, while advanced users can access Python notebooks to reproduce or adapt the underlying analyses. An in-app guide takes new users through the main features.

Powered by cloud-optimised data storage, ERA Explorer calculates statistics in just a few seconds from decades of hourly data, delivering rapid insights while keeping the full scientific dataset openly accessible through the CDS.

C3S Atlas: interactive climate projections and reproducible insights

For users seeking to explore future climate change trends alongside historical data, the Copernicus Interactive Climate Atlas (C3S Atlas) delivers global climate projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP), an international scientific programme which brings together climate models from research centres worldwide to produce standardised projections of past and future climate conditions, as well as ERA5 reanalysis and regional downscaled datasets. Since its launch in 2024, it has become a central tool for examining climate projections, observations, and reanalysis data at global and regional scales.

Key variables include mean temperature, accumulated precipitation, and extreme heat days. Regional time series are among the most popular products, complemented by long-term climatological averages and change maps across different periods and global warming levels.

C3S Atlas visualisation showing the rate of change in summer (JJA) daily mean temperature (°C per decade) in 1991–2020, based on ERA5-Land data. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Climate Change Service/ECMWF.

Data can be downloaded directly in different formats, supporting both the communication of results to non-specialist audiences as well as in-depth scientific analysis. A recent addition, the C3S Atlas User Tools, improves transparency and reproducibility through Jupyter notebooks and an open GitHub repository, allowing users to recreate indices and visualisations.

With extensive guidance, a helpful video tutorial, and built-in support, the C3S Atlas serves policymakers, researchers, journalists, and educators seeking authoritative and accessible climate intelligence.

Methane Hotspot Explorer: tracking major methane plumes from space

Turning to atmospheric composition, the Methane Hotspot Explorer from CAMS focuses on methane, a short-lived greenhouse gas contributing to near-term warming. The app visualises large methane emission plumes detected using satellite observations from the TROPOMI instrument aboard the Copernicus Sentinel-5P satellite.

The Explorer uses machine-learning techniques to identify these emission plumes. These are displayed on a dynamic global map with estimated upwind source locations and indicative source categories such as coal, oil, gas, or solid waste, based on emission inventories.

Through the Methane Hotspot Explorer app, users can view methane plumes from strongly emitting sources in near real time, explore detailed maps, and access data on the estimated location and magnitude of emission sources. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service/ECMWF.

While the kilometre-scale resolution does not allow individual facilities to be pinpointed, the tool shows where major emission events are occurring worldwide. Recent upgrades now provide detailed plume shape visualisations, illustrating how atmospheric conditions influence the spread and transport of methane. By transforming satellite data into an accessible monitoring platform, the Methane Hotspot Explorer supports greater transparency of a crucial driver of climate change.

Aerosol Alerts: early warnings for major air pollution events

The CAMS Aerosol Alerts service provides daily, forward-looking insights into major aerosol events affecting air quality worldwide. Launched in 2024, the free tool delivers forecasts up to three days in advance, helping users anticipate aerosol episodes linked to dust storms, wildfire smoke, and severe pollution outbreaks.

Built on ECMWF’s Integrated Forecasting System, the service evaluates aerosol optical depth and surface concentrations of particulate matter with diameters of 10 and 2.5 micrometres against reference levels and predefined thresholds. When anomalies exceed these limits, the system triggers high, very high or extreme alerts.

Visualisation of Aerosol Alerts forecast with the CAMS global system at 12:00 UTC on 26 February, 2026. Credit: European Union, Copernicus Atmosphere Monitoring Service/ECMWF.

The interactive homepage features a global map displaying multiple aerosol species simultaneously, including dust, sea salt, sulphate, organic matter, and black carbon, alongside animated wind patterns showing atmospheric transport. Users can customise views, compare forecasts with satellite observations, explore 2D or 3D perspectives, and review forecast performance by region and season.

A personalised notification system allows users to define areas of interest and receive automated email alerts, helping public authorities, health professionals, and individuals to better respond to air pollution events.

Powering the apps: cloud-ready data and a growing ecosystem

Behind these applications lies a data infrastructure built around the Climate Data Store and Atmosphere Data Store. These platforms provide free and open access to large collections of quality-assured climate and atmospheric datasets, spanning reanalysis, observations, projections, and near-real-time forecasts.

The integration of Analysis Ready, Cloud Optimised (ARCO) datasets is enabling interactive analysis and visualisation directly in the cloud, allowing apps to deliver rapid insight directly from the underlying data stores without heavy data transfers.

To showcase this expanding ecosystem, Copernicus has launched a dedicated Data Stores Applications Catalogue with a selection of interactive tools featuring data from C3S, CAMS, and the Copernicus Emergency Management Service (CEMS). The repository brings together a wide selection of Copernicus-enabled tools and provides access to new apps built on Copernicus data.

Together, these digital tools illustrate how open and FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) environmental data can be transformed into accessible, user-friendly, tools and services, strengthening transparency, supporting decision-making, and bringing climate and air-quality intelligence closer to society.
articles.reasoning: The article describes five innovative Copernicus applications that transform complex climate and atmospheric data into accessible tools with broad societal benefits. These apps support understanding, risk assessment, and decision-making on climate change, heat stress, methane emissions, and air pollution globally. The story highlights significant positive real-world impact by improving transparency and enabling informed responses to environmental challenges, with detailed context and examples.
articles.personality_title: Five Copernicus apps make climate and air quality data easy to understand
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Copernicus, a European environmental program, collects a huge amount of climate and air quality data from satellites and weather models. This data is very detailed but can be hard for most people to use directly.

**What happened** – Copernicus has developed five new digital tools that turn complex climate and atmosphere data into simple, interactive maps and charts. These apps include Thermal Trace for tracking heat and cold stress, ERA Explorer for exploring 85 years of global climate data, C3S Atlas for future climate projections, Methane Hotspot Explorer to spot big methane leaks from space, and Aerosol Alerts for early warnings about air pollution events.

**Impact** – By making data easy to access and understand, these apps help many users like journalists, public health officials, planners, and researchers. For example, Thermal Trace uses a “feels-like” temperature index to better show heat risks, and Methane Hotspot Explorer reveals major sources of methane, a strong greenhouse gas. Aerosol Alerts warn about dust storms or wildfire smoke days ahead, giving people time to prepare. These tools support better decisions on climate and health.

**What's next step** – Copernicus will keep improving these apps and add more tools in a new online catalogue. The data behind the apps is stored in the cloud, letting users quickly explore and download information. This growing digital ecosystem will help more people and organizations around the world use environmental data to understand and respond to climate and air quality challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Five new Copernicus apps turn complex climate and air pollution data into easy-to-use tools that support informed decisions worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Cinq applications Copernicus facilitent la compréhension des données climatiques et de la qualité de l'air
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Copernicus, un programme environnemental européen, collecte une grande quantité de données sur le climat et la qualité de l'air grâce à des satellites et des modèles météorologiques. Ces données sont très détaillées mais difficiles à utiliser directement pour la plupart des gens.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Copernicus a développé cinq nouveaux outils numériques qui transforment ces données complexes en cartes et graphiques interactifs simples à comprendre. Parmi ces applications, Thermal Trace suit le stress thermique, ERA Explorer explore 85 ans de données climatiques mondiales, C3S Atlas présente des projections climatiques futures, Methane Hotspot Explorer détecte les fuites majeures de méthane depuis l'espace, et Aerosol Alerts alerte à l'avance sur les épisodes de pollution atmosphérique.

**Impact** – En rendant les données accessibles et compréhensibles, ces applications aident de nombreux utilisateurs comme les journalistes, les professionnels de santé, les urbanistes et les chercheurs. Par exemple, Thermal Trace utilise un indice de température ressentie pour mieux montrer les risques liés à la chaleur, et Methane Hotspot Explorer localise les sources majeures de méthane, un puissant gaz à effet de serre. Aerosol Alerts prévient des tempêtes de poussière ou des fumées de feux de forêt plusieurs jours à l’avance, permettant de mieux se préparer. Ces outils soutiennent de meilleures décisions sur le climat et la santé.

**Prochaines étapes** – Copernicus continuera d'améliorer ces applications et d'ajouter de nouveaux outils dans un nouveau catalogue en ligne. Les données utilisées sont stockées dans le cloud, permettant aux utilisateurs d’explorer et de télécharger rapidement les informations. Cet écosystème numérique en expansion aidera davantage de personnes et d’organisations dans le monde à utiliser les données environnementales pour comprendre et agir face aux défis climatiques et de qualité de l’air.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Cinq nouvelles applications Copernicus transforment des données complexes sur le climat et la pollution en outils faciles à utiliser pour des décisions éclairées dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Cinco aplicaciones de Copernicus facilitan el acceso a datos climáticos y de calidad del aire
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Copernicus, un programa ambiental europeo, recopila una gran cantidad de datos sobre el clima y la calidad del aire a través de satélites y modelos meteorológicos. Estos datos son muy detallados pero difíciles de usar directamente para la mayoría de las personas.

**Qué pasó** – Copernicus ha creado cinco nuevas herramientas digitales que convierten estos datos complejos en mapas y gráficos interactivos fáciles de entender. Entre ellas están Thermal Trace para monitorear el estrés por calor y frío, ERA Explorer que explora 85 años de datos climáticos globales, C3S Atlas con proyecciones climáticas futuras, Methane Hotspot Explorer para detectar grandes emisiones de metano desde el espacio, y Aerosol Alerts que avisa con anticipación sobre episodios de contaminación del aire.

**Impacto** – Al hacer los datos accesibles y comprensibles, estas apps ayudan a muchos usuarios como periodistas, profesionales de salud, planificadores e investigadores. Por ejemplo, Thermal Trace usa un índice de temperatura “sensación térmica” para mostrar mejor los riesgos de calor, y Methane Hotspot Explorer muestra las principales fuentes de metano, un gas de efecto invernadero potente. Aerosol Alerts advierte sobre tormentas de polvo o humo de incendios con días de anticipación, dando tiempo para prepararse. Estas herramientas apoyan mejores decisiones sobre clima y salud.

**Próximo paso** – Copernicus seguirá mejorando estas aplicaciones y añadirá más herramientas en un nuevo catálogo en línea. Los datos están almacenados en la nube, lo que permite a los usuarios explorar y descargar información rápidamente. Este creciente ecosistema digital ayudará a más personas y organizaciones en todo el mundo a usar datos ambientales para entender y responder a los desafíos climáticos y de calidad del aire.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cinco nuevas aplicaciones de Copernicus convierten datos complejos sobre clima y contaminación en herramientas fáciles de usar que apoyan decisiones informadas a nivel mundial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing globe surrounded by five translucent, floating digital screens displaying stylized interactive maps and data visualizations of heat stress, climate projections, methane plumes, and aerosol alerts, set against a calm sky with soft natural colors, symbolizing accessible climate and air-quality intelligence transforming global understanding.

articles.title: Georgia’s forestry industry is in crisis. One solution could be in your medicine cabinet.
articles.content:
This coverage is made possible through a partnership between Grist and WABE , Atlanta’s NPR station.

Georgia is one of the leading states for forestry, ranking first in annual harvest volume and the export of forest products. The state’s forests that are managed for a commercial product offset about a third of Georgia’s greenhouse gas emissions. But a recent rash of paper mill closures coming on the heels of Hurricane Helene, which decimated trees across much of Georgia in 2024, has brought the industry to a crisis point.

Last week, in the final days of Georgia’s legislative session, lawmakers passed several bills to help the state’s struggling forestry industry. Among them is a measure that would allow forest landowners who get the state’s conservation tax benefits to also take part in carbon markets. (Researchers and industry groups are working to establish a Georgia-specific carbon credit market.) They also passed a tax credit update aimed at attracting forestry manufacturers to Georgia and a bill to block local governments from banning mobile sawmills on agricultural land. Another legislative effort, a bill to eliminate sales tax on timber harvesting, cleared the state House but failed to pass the Senate.

Most of Georgia’s forests — about 92 percent — are privately owned, and most owners are individuals or families, not large corporations. That means decisions about how to manage the land, including whether to replant trees after harvesting or after a hurricane, come down to what makes financial sense for individuals who are often counting on their trees to fund college tuition or retirement. Paper mill closures diminished a key source of revenue for those landowners, and if they can’t make money from the trees themselves, industry experts warn, they may sell the land to developers.

“Forest owners have a tremendous amount of pressure on them to give in to urban growth or to turn over their land to maybe an annual crop,” said Chris Luettgen, who works in the Renewable Bioproducts Institute at Georgia Tech. “But some of this land is perfect for trees and not really too easy to grow a crop on. And so they’re kind of stuck.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The state budget also set aside nearly $9 million for research into replacing fossil fuel byproducts with wood pulp to make things like textiles and pharmaceuticals. Governor Brian Kemp has already signed the budget bill, including the research funds. He has until May 12 to sign or veto the other bills.

The research money will fund the initiative that Luettgen is working on at Georgia Tech. He and his team will take the material that’s used to make paper and cardboard — sawdust, bark, and chips left over from cutting trees down into neat pieces of lumber — and develop uses that can be scaled up.

One such product is acetaminophen, the drug better known as Tylenol, which is derived from petrochemicals that are separated from crude oil as it’s processed into gasoline. Researchers have demonstrated in the lab that the necessary fossil fuel molecule can be replaced with one derived from wood, Luettgen said. They’re also working on a replacement for nylon, which also comes from fossil fuels.

“Things that typically have come from fossil fuels and the cracking of crude oil, we believe we can instead make out of wood-based products,” said Luettgen.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

But all of this research is happening in small quantities. The next step is to figure out if it works at a larger scale, which the influx of state funding will help pay for.

“It means that we can take it out of the lab and put it into real, not quite commercial, but a real upscaled operation and demonstrate its capabilities,” said Luettgen.
articles.reasoning: The article describes significant legislative and research efforts in Georgia to support the forestry industry amid crisis, including innovative environmental solutions like developing wood-based alternatives to fossil fuel-derived products. This has broad environmental and economic benefits, supporting sustainable forestry, reducing greenhouse gas emissions, and advancing renewable materials at scale.
articles.personality_title: Georgia supports forestry with new laws and wood-based research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Georgia is a top state for forestry, with many privately owned forests that provide wood products and help reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Recently, the industry faced a crisis because paper mills closed and a hurricane damaged many trees in 2024.

**What happened** – In response, Georgia lawmakers passed bills to help forest owners and attract forestry businesses. These laws allow landowners to earn money from carbon credits, offer tax benefits for forestry manufacturers, and protect mobile sawmills on farms. The state also set aside nearly $9 million to research using wood to replace fossil fuels in products like medicine and textiles.

**Impact** – These efforts support family forest owners who rely on trees for income and help prevent land from being sold for development. The research aims to create wood-based alternatives to products like acetaminophen (Tylenol) and nylon, which usually come from oil. This could reduce pollution and create new markets for wood.

**What's next step** – Researchers will use the new funds to test their wood-based products on a larger scale outside the lab. If successful, this could lead to real-world production and more sustainable industries in Georgia.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Georgia is taking important steps to help its forestry industry recover by passing supportive laws and funding innovative research to turn wood into useful products typically made from fossil fuels.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Géorgie soutient la sylviculture avec de nouvelles lois et la recherche sur les produits à base de bois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Géorgie est un état leader en sylviculture, avec de nombreuses forêts privées qui fournissent des produits en bois et aident à réduire les émissions de gaz à effet de serre. Récemment, l’industrie a connu une crise à cause de la fermeture de papeteries et des dégâts causés par un ouragan en 2024.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En réponse, les législateurs de Géorgie ont adopté des lois pour aider les propriétaires forestiers et attirer les entreprises sylvicoles. Ces lois permettent aux propriétaires de gagner de l’argent grâce aux crédits carbone, offrent des avantages fiscaux aux fabricants forestiers et protègent les scieries mobiles sur les terres agricoles. L’État a aussi réservé près de 9 millions de dollars pour la recherche sur l’utilisation du bois pour remplacer les produits issus des énergies fossiles, comme les médicaments et les textiles.

**Impact** – Ces mesures soutiennent les propriétaires familiaux de forêts qui dépendent des arbres pour leurs revenus et aident à éviter que les terres soient vendues pour construire des bâtiments. La recherche vise à créer des alternatives à base de bois pour des produits comme l’acétaminophène (Tylenol) et le nylon, qui viennent normalement du pétrole. Cela pourrait réduire la pollution et créer de nouveaux marchés pour le bois.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront les fonds pour tester leurs produits à base de bois à plus grande échelle, hors laboratoire. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait mener à une production réelle et à des industries plus durables en Géorgie.

**Phrase clé** – La Géorgie prend des mesures importantes pour aider sa sylviculture à se redresser en adoptant des lois favorables et en finançant la recherche innovante pour transformer le bois en produits habituellement fabriqués à partir de combustibles fossiles.
articles.personality_title_es: Georgia apoya la silvicultura con nuevas leyes e investigación basada en la madera
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Georgia es uno de los estados líderes en silvicultura, con muchos bosques privados que producen madera y ayudan a reducir las emisiones de gases de efecto invernadero. Recientemente, la industria enfrentó una crisis por el cierre de fábricas de papel y un huracán que dañó muchos árboles en 2024.

**Qué pasó** – En respuesta, los legisladores de Georgia aprobaron leyes para ayudar a los dueños de bosques y atraer empresas forestales. Estas leyes permiten que los propietarios ganen dinero con créditos de carbono, ofrecen beneficios fiscales a fabricantes forestales y protegen los aserraderos móviles en tierras agrícolas. El estado también destinó casi 9 millones de dólares para investigar cómo usar la madera para reemplazar productos derivados de combustibles fósiles, como medicinas y textiles.

**Impacto** – Estas acciones apoyan a los dueños familiares de bosques que dependen de los árboles para sus ingresos y ayudan a evitar que vendan sus tierras para desarrollos urbanos. La investigación busca crear alternativas a base de madera para productos como el acetaminofén (Tylenol) y el nylon, que normalmente provienen del petróleo. Esto podría reducir la contaminación y crear nuevos mercados para la madera.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán los fondos para probar sus productos de madera a mayor escala fuera del laboratorio. Si tienen éxito, esto podría llevar a una producción real y a industrias más sostenibles en Georgia.

**Frase clave** – Georgia está dando pasos importantes para ayudar a su industria forestal a recuperarse mediante leyes de apoyo y financiamiento a investigaciones innovadoras que transforman la madera en productos que usualmente se hacen con combustibles fósiles.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene Georgia forest with neatly stacked lumber and sawdust subtly transforming into glowing chemical structures shaped like medicine capsules and textile fibers, symbolizing wood-based pharmaceuticals and fabrics, set against a soft natural color palette with gentle sunlight filtering through the trees.

articles.title: Five bird species missing for decades were found in 2025 thanks to citizen birders
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM In February 2026, two French birders in Chad photographed a rusty bush lark. The species had not been recorded in 94 years. It was the most dramatic entry in a year of rediscoveries that have brought the Lost Birds List from 163 species down to 120 since 2022. The […]
The post Five bird species missing for decades were found in 2025 thanks to citizen birders first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

In February 2026, two French birders in Chad photographed a rusty bush lark. The species had not been recorded in 94 years. It was the most dramatic entry in a year of rediscoveries that have brought the Lost Birds List from 163 species down to 120 since 2022.

The list tracks birds not photographed, recorded, or genetically detected for at least a decade. It is maintained by the Search for Lost Birds project, a partnership between the American Bird Conservancy, Re:wild, and BirdLife International. John Mittermeier, the project’s director, describes it as an “early warning system” for species that might “potentially slip between the cracks” of slower, more formal conservation assessments.

Five birds come back

All five 2025 rediscoveries came from Southeast Asia and the Pacific, and all were made by individual birders and photographers, not formal research teams.

Papua New Guinean ornithologist John Lamaris photographed the Bismarck kingfisher in May 2025, the first record in 13 years. In neighboring Indonesian Papua, Ethan Skinner documented the Biak myzomela, a honeyeater found only on the islands of Biak and Supiori, for the first time in two decades. The broad-billed fairywren turned up in the same region: birder Daniel Hoops and his tour guide Royke Mananta photographed it and recorded its song, 11 years after the last observation.

Two more turned up in the Philippines. Shareef Khaddafi photographed the Sulu cuckooshrike in the Sulu Archipelago, the first image in 18 years. Birding guide Martin Kennewell photographed the rufous-breasted blue flycatcher in the tropical lowlands of Luzon Island, last seen in 2008.

One more potential find is still unconfirmed. Harish Thangaraj recorded sounds he believes belong to Jerdon’s courser, a nocturnal bird from South India that hasn’t been documented in 125 years. Scientists need photographs and additional recordings before they can make it official.

Who is doing the finding

Mittermeier and his team scan public birding platforms each year, including eBird, iNaturalist, and Xeno-Canto, for observations of species on the list. Most of the finds come through those channels. “The most fun part for me of this whole initiative and experience is seeing these discoveries that people around the world are making,” he said.

The list is designed to “fill conservation data gaps” before a species disappears, Mittermeier says, getting ahead of formal assessment systems that take longer to catch up.

One gone, one reclassified

In 2025, scientists declared the slender-billed curlew extinct. Last documented in 1995, the migratory shorebird was steadily worn down by expanding farmland, degraded wetlands, and hunting of a species that reproduced slowly. The declaration means resources stop going toward a bird that is no longer out there.

Genetic analysis removed another species for a different reason. The white-chested tinkerbird from Zambia, known from a single specimen collected in 1964, was reclassified as a subspecies of the yellow-rumped tinkerbird rather than a species of its own.

Mittermeier sees both outcomes as useful. Confirming extinction means “we’re not putting effort into looking for something that isn’t there and doesn’t exist.” Resolving the genetics sharpens what conservation dollars are actually protecting.

Six species added, all on islands

Six new species join the list in 2026, and every one is an island bird. They include the critically endangered Mindoro bleeding-heart, last photographed in 2005, and the Mindoro imperial pigeon, last documented in 2016, both from the Philippine island of Mindoro. Also newly listed are the Guadalcanal honeyeater from the Solomon Islands; the Minahasa shortwing from Sulawesi, Indonesia, one of the world’s few bird species never photographed; the Samoan white-eye from the highlands of Savai’i; and the Vanikoro white-eye from a remote island in the southeastern Solomon Islands.

Island birds have no fallback when conditions deteriorate. They face threats that continental birds can sometimes outrun: invasive predators, rising seas, storms growing more intense as oceans warm. “We know islands are at the forefront of extinction, and so having lost birds on small islands [is] a little bit concerning to me,” Mittermeier said.

The list has dropped by about 25 percent in five years, driven largely by individual birders working through public platforms. That trajectory is what Mittermeier is counting on. “I’m really hopeful that we can get this list down to zero,” he said. “I think that’s feasible … given the power and the interest of this global community.”

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports the rediscovery of five bird species previously missing for decades, thanks to citizen birders globally. This represents a significant positive impact on biodiversity conservation, highlighting successful community-driven efforts to track and protect endangered species. The story focuses on a clear, meaningful environmental achievement with broad implications for conservation and ecological awareness.
articles.personality_title: Five long-lost bird species rediscovered by citizen birders in 2025
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Some bird species disappear from sight for many years, making scientists worry they might be extinct. A special list called the Lost Birds List keeps track of birds not seen or recorded for at least ten years. This list helps conservation groups watch out for birds that might be in danger.

**What happened** – In 2025, five bird species missing for over a decade were found again, all in Southeast Asia and the Pacific. These discoveries were made by everyday bird watchers, not scientists. For example, in May 2025, a kingfisher in Papua New Guinea was seen for the first time in 13 years. Other birds, like the Biak myzomela and the rufous-breasted blue flycatcher, were also spotted after many years.

**Impact** – These rediscoveries show how people around the world can help protect nature by watching and sharing what they find. It also means these birds still have a chance to survive, and conservation efforts can focus on helping them. Finding these birds early helps prevent them from disappearing completely.

**What's next step** – Scientists will keep checking public platforms where bird watchers share photos and sounds to find more lost birds. They also need more proof, like photos, to confirm other possible rediscoveries. Meanwhile, the Lost Birds List will be updated to help focus conservation where it is most needed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Thanks to citizen bird watchers, five bird species missing for many years were found again in 2025, giving new hope for their protection.
articles.personality_title_fr: Cinq espèces d’oiseaux disparues depuis des décennies retrouvées par des ornithologues amateurs en 2025
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Certaines espèces d’oiseaux disparaissent de la vue pendant de nombreuses années, ce qui inquiète les scientifiques qui craignent leur extinction. Une liste spéciale appelée Liste des oiseaux perdus recense les oiseaux non vus ou enregistrés depuis au moins dix ans. Cette liste aide les groupes de conservation à surveiller les oiseaux en danger.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2025, cinq espèces d’oiseaux absentes depuis plus d’une décennie ont été retrouvées, toutes en Asie du Sud-Est et dans le Pacifique. Ces découvertes ont été faites par des ornithologues amateurs, pas par des scientifiques. Par exemple, en mai 2025, un martin-pêcheur en Papouasie-Nouvelle-Guinée a été observé pour la première fois depuis 13 ans. D’autres oiseaux, comme le myzomèle de Biak et le gobemouche bleu à poitrine roux, ont aussi été repérés après de nombreuses années.

**Impact** – Ces redécouvertes montrent comment des personnes du monde entier peuvent aider à protéger la nature en observant et partageant leurs trouvailles. Cela signifie aussi que ces oiseaux ont encore une chance de survivre, et les efforts de conservation peuvent se concentrer pour les aider. Trouver ces oiseaux tôt aide à éviter leur disparition totale.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront de vérifier les plateformes publiques où les ornithologues amateurs partagent photos et sons pour retrouver d’autres oiseaux perdus. Ils ont aussi besoin de plus de preuves, comme des photos, pour confirmer d’autres possibles redécouvertes. En attendant, la Liste des oiseaux perdus sera mise à jour pour mieux orienter la conservation.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce aux ornithologues amateurs, cinq espèces d’oiseaux disparues depuis longtemps ont été retrouvées en 2025, offrant un nouvel espoir pour leur protection.
articles.personality_title_es: Cinco especies de aves desaparecidas por décadas fueron redescubiertas por observadores en 2025
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Algunas especies de aves desaparecen de la vista durante muchos años, lo que preocupa a los científicos que temen que estén extintas. Una lista especial llamada Lista de Aves Perdidas registra las aves que no se han visto o grabado durante al menos diez años. Esta lista ayuda a los grupos de conservación a vigilar a las aves en peligro.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, cinco especies de aves que no se habían visto en más de una década fueron encontradas nuevamente, todas en el sudeste asiático y el Pacífico. Estos descubrimientos fueron hechos por observadores de aves aficionados, no por científicos. Por ejemplo, en mayo de 2025, se vio un martín pescador en Papúa Nueva Guinea por primera vez en 13 años. Otras aves, como el myzómela de Biak y el papamoscas azul pechirrubio, también fueron avistadas después de muchos años.

**Impacto** – Estos redescubrimientos muestran cómo personas de todo el mundo pueden ayudar a proteger la naturaleza observando y compartiendo lo que encuentran. También significa que estas aves todavía tienen una oportunidad de sobrevivir, y los esfuerzos de conservación pueden centrarse en ayudarlas. Encontrar estas aves temprano ayuda a evitar que desaparezcan por completo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán revisando plataformas públicas donde los observadores de aves comparten fotos y sonidos para encontrar más aves perdidas. También necesitan más pruebas, como fotos, para confirmar otros posibles redescubrimientos. Mientras tanto, la Lista de Aves Perdidas se actualizará para enfocar mejor la conservación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Gracias a los observadores de aves aficionados, cinco especies de aves desaparecidas por muchos años fueron redescubiertas en 2025, dando nueva esperanza para su protección.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of five distinct, vividly colored birds—each representing the rediscovered species from Southeast Asia and the Pacific—perched gently on intertwined branches that form a delicate globe shape, symbolizing global citizen efforts, set against a soft, natural palette of greens, browns, and muted blues, with subtle silhouettes of camera lenses and sound waves blending into the background to evoke birders’ photography and recordings.
